revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''やんさんま祭り'''は、[[富山県]][[射水市]][[下村]]加茂の[[加茂神社 (射水市)|下村加茂神社]]にて毎年5月4日に行われ...'
revision.text:
'''やんさんま祭り'''は、[[富山県]][[射水市]][[下村]]加茂の[[加茂神社 (射水市)|下村加茂神社]]にて毎年5月4日に行われる[[流鏑馬]]行事である。3日には宵祭が5日には裏祭が行われる。
== 概要 == 京都[[加茂神社]]から勧請された下村加茂神社創建当時から始められたと云われており、「やんさんま」とは流鏑馬が訛ったもので、[[京都]][[上賀茂神社]]で行われている[[競馬会神事]]より伝わったものといわれる。また'''牛乗式（別名 牛つぶし）'''という全国でもここだけの神事が行われており、[[1967年]]（[[昭和]]42年）3月25日に富山県の[[無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。
== 主な日程 == 3日の宵祭りには、宵祭走馬の儀、宵祭の祭式、宵祭九遍式、宵祭流鏑馬の儀の順に行われ、[[獅子舞]]の舞始祭も行われていたが、現在は宵祭走馬の儀と宵祭流鏑馬の儀が略式で行われている。
4日の本祭は、朝8時より悪魔祓いの獅子舞の舞いから始まり、神馬・走馬の儀、御戸開きの儀、神幸式、神馬式、御旅所の儀、牛乗式と続く。その後も還御、例祭式、九遍式と続き最後に流鏑馬式が行われる。
5日の裏祭は、午後2時頃より舞納祭（獅子舞）、例祭成就報賽祭が執行され、すべての諸行事が終了となる。
== 牛乗式（牛つぶし） == 鞍を載せた1頭の牡牛に、[[兜]]に[[甲冑]]を纏い赤面の大鼻の面を被り武者（田の神）となった若者が乗る。神の依代は兜の中天に挿した和紙でできた牡丹の花である。手には1[[丈]]3[[尺]]（約3.94m）の弓を持ち、背中には矢を背負い境内に入ると「天下泰平」、「五穀成就」を祈願して拝殿の屋根（北方）に向け一矢を放つ。その後神幸御順路に入ると多くの若衆が牛に駆け寄り牛を押さえつける。牛は嫌がって暴れるが多くの若衆が交代で押えつけるため、やがて疲労し地面に押し付けられ座り込まされてしまうというものである。この神事は田の神が乗った牛を座らせ留め置き、この地の五穀豊穣をいつまでも願うものと共に、雨乞い行事も付せて行われているとも云われている。
== 流鏑馬式 == 騎手は、紅白の[[御幣]]を中天に付けた荒目に編んだ龍笠に、白紙の[[紙垂]]を着け、紫[[縮緬]]の鉢巻、緋羅紗の[[襦袢]]に白縮緬で襷掛けをし、黒[[ベルベット|天鵞絨（ベルベット）]]の丸帯に乗馬袴、手には黒の手覆いに足先には白足袋姿で馬にまたがる。
3人の騎手は拝殿西側から次々と杉並木の参道を駆け抜け、参道内に設けられた高さ約10mにある大的に向け、それぞれ3回ずつ矢を射る。牛乗式及び流鏑馬式で放たれた矢は観客たちが奪い合うように拾い、五穀豊穣や家内繁栄などを祈り「除魔招福」の霊符として家に持ち帰り神棚に供える。
== 関連項目 == * [[加茂神社 (射水市)|下村加茂神社]] * [[流鏑馬]] * [[無形民俗文化財]] * [[下村]]
== 参考文献 == *『祭礼事典・富山県』（富山県祭礼研究会 編・[[おうふう|桜楓社]]）[[1991年]]（[[平成]]3年）12月25日発行 ISBN4-273-02481-0 *『祭礼行事・富山県』（高橋秀雄・漆間元三 編・桜楓社）1991年（平成3年）10月25日発行 ISBN4-273-02482-9 *『北陸の祭り』（企画 [[北陸電力]]・チューエツ メディア制作室出版部）[[2001年]]（平成13年）3月23日発行 ISBN4-9980730-2-8
== 外部リンク == *[http://www4.tkc.pref.toyama.jp/video/ とやまデジタル映像ライブラリー]（紹介動画あり）
{{DEFAULTSORT:やんさんままつり}} {{event-stub}} [[Category:富山県の祭り]] [[Category:富山県の文化]] [[Category:射水市]]

'''やんさんま祭り'''は、[[富山県]][[射水市]][[下村]]加茂の[[加茂神社 (射水市)|下村加茂神社]]にて毎年5月4日に行われる[[流鏑馬]]行事である。3日には宵祭が5日には裏祭が行われる。
== 概要 == 京都[[加茂神社]]から勧請された下村加茂神社創建当時から始められたと云われており、「やんさんま」とは流鏑馬が訛ったもので、[[京都]][[上賀茂神社]]で行われている[[競馬会神事]]より伝わったものといわれる。また、'''牛乗式（牛つぶし）'''という全国でもここだけの神事が行われており、[[1967年]]（[[昭和]]42年）3月25日に富山県の[[無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。なお[[2006年]]（[[平成]]18年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの祭り百選部門）」に選定されている。
== 主な日程 == 3日の宵祭りには、宵祭走馬の儀、宵祭の祭式、宵祭九遍式、宵祭流鏑馬の儀の順に行われ、[[獅子舞]]の舞始祭も行われていたが、現在は宵祭走馬の儀と宵祭流鏑馬の儀が略式で行われている。
4日の本祭は、朝8時より悪魔祓いの獅子舞の舞いから始まり、神馬・走馬の儀、御戸開きの儀、神幸式、神馬式、御旅所の儀、牛乗式と続く。その後も還御、例祭式、九遍式と続き最後に流鏑馬式が行われる。
5日の裏祭は、午後2時頃より舞納祭（獅子舞）、例祭成就報賽祭が執行され、すべての諸行事が終了となる。
== 牛乗式（牛つぶし） == 鞍を載せた1頭の牡牛に、[[兜]]に[[甲冑]]を纏って赤面の大鼻の面を被り、武者（田の神）となった若者が乗る。神の依代は、兜の中天に挿した和紙でできた牡丹の花である。手には1[[丈]]3[[尺]]（約3.94メートル）の弓を持ち、背中には矢を背負って境内に入ると、「天下泰平」「五穀成就」を祈願して拝殿の屋根（北方）に向け、一矢を放つ。その後に神幸御順路へ入ると、多くの若衆が牛に駆け寄って押さえつける。牛は嫌がって暴れるが、多くの若衆が交代で押えつけるため、やがて疲労して地面に押し付けられ、座り込まされてしまうというものである。この神事は田の神が乗った牛を座らせて留め置き、この地の五穀豊穣をいつまでも願うと共に、雨乞い行事も併せて行われているとも伝えられている。
== 流鏑馬式 == 騎手は、紅白の[[御幣]]を中天に付けた荒目に編んだ龍笠に白紙の[[紙垂]]を着け、紫[[縮緬]]の鉢巻、緋羅紗の[[襦袢]]に白縮緬で襷掛けをし、黒[[ベルベット|天鵞絨（ベルベット）]]の丸帯に乗馬袴、手には黒の手覆いに足先には白足袋姿で馬にまたがる。
3人の騎手は拝殿西側から次々と杉並木の参道を駆け抜け、参道内に設けられた高さ約10メートルにある大的に向け、それぞれ3回ずつ矢を射る。牛乗式及び流鏑馬式で放たれた矢は観客たちが奪い合うように拾い、五穀豊穣や家内繁栄などを祈って「除魔招福」の霊符として家に持ち帰り、神棚に供える。
== 関連項目 == * [[加茂神社 (射水市)|下村加茂神社]] * [[流鏑馬]] * [[無形民俗文化財]] * [[日本の祭一覧]] * [[下村]]
== 参考文献 == * 『祭礼事典・富山県』（富山県祭礼研究会 編・[[おうふう|桜楓社]]）[[1991年]]（平成3年）12月25日発行 ISBN 4-273-02481-0 * 『祭礼行事・富山県』（高橋秀雄・漆間元三 編・桜楓社）1991年（平成3年）10月25日発行 ISBN 4-273-02482-9 * 『北陸の祭り』（企画 [[北陸電力]]・チューエツ メディア制作室出版部）[[2001年]]（平成13年）3月23日発行 ISBN 4-9980730-2-8 * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』（富山県[[教育委員会]] 生涯学習・文化財室）[[2007年]]（平成19年）3月発行
== 外部リンク == * [http://www4.tkc.pref.toyama.jp/video/ とやまデジタル映像ライブラリー]（紹介動画あり）

'''やんさんま祭り'''は、[[富山県]][[射水市]][[下村]]加茂の[[加茂神社 (射水市)|下村加茂神社]]にて毎年5月4日に行われる[[流鏑馬]]行事である。3日には宵祭が5日には裏祭が行われる。
== 概要 == 京都[[加茂神社]]から勧請された下村加茂神社創建当時から始められたと云われており、「やんさんま」とは流鏑馬が訛ったもので、[[京都]][[上賀茂神社]]で行われている[[競馬会神事]]より伝わったものといわれる。また、'''牛乗式（牛つぶし）'''という全国でもここだけの神事が行われており、[[1967年]]（[[昭和]]42年）3月25日に富山県の[[無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。なお[[2006年]]（[[平成]]18年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの祭り百選部門）」に選定されている。
== 主な日程 == 3日の宵祭りには、宵祭走馬の儀、宵祭の祭式、宵祭九遍式、宵祭流鏑馬の儀の順に行われ、[[獅子舞]]の舞始祭も行われていたが、現在は宵祭走馬の儀と宵祭流鏑馬の儀が略式で行われている。
4日の本祭は、朝8時より悪魔祓いの獅子舞の舞いから始まり、神馬・走馬の儀、御戸開きの儀、神幸式、神馬式、御旅所の儀、牛乗式と続く。その後も還御、例祭式、九遍式と続き最後に流鏑馬式が行われる。
5日の裏祭は、午後2時頃より舞納祭（獅子舞）、例祭成就報賽祭が執行され、すべての諸行事が終了となる。
== 牛乗式（牛つぶし） == 鞍を載せた1頭の牡牛に、[[兜]]に[[甲冑]]を纏って赤面の大鼻の面を被り、武者（田の神）となった若者が乗る。神の依代は、兜の中天に挿した和紙でできた牡丹の花である。手には1[[丈]]3[[尺]]（約3.94メートル）の弓を持ち、背中には矢を背負って境内に入ると、「天下泰平」「五穀成就」を祈願して拝殿の屋根（北方）に向け、一矢を放つ。その後に神幸御順路へ入ると、多くの若衆が牛に駆け寄って押さえつける。牛は嫌がって暴れるが、多くの若衆が交代で押えつけるため、やがて疲労して地面に押し付けられ、座り込まされてしまうというものである。この神事は田の神が乗った牛を座らせて留め置き、この地の五穀豊穣をいつまでも願うと共に、雨乞い行事も併せて行われているとも伝えられている。
== 流鏑馬式 == 騎手は、紅白の[[御幣]]を中天に付けた荒目に編んだ龍笠に白紙の[[紙垂]]を着け、紫[[縮緬]]の鉢巻、緋羅紗の[[襦袢]]に白縮緬で襷掛けをし、黒[[ベルベット|天鵞絨（ベルベット）]]の丸帯に乗馬袴、手には黒の手覆いに足先には白足袋姿で馬にまたがる。
3人の騎手は拝殿西側から次々と杉並木の参道を駆け抜け、参道内に設けられた高さ約10メートルにある大的に向け、それぞれ3回ずつ矢を射る。牛乗式及び流鏑馬式で放たれた矢は観客たちが奪い合うように拾い、五穀豊穣や家内繁栄などを祈って「除魔招福」の霊符として家に持ち帰り、神棚に供える。
== 獅子舞 == 獅子舞は小さな獅子頭を[[紋付]]・[[袴]]姿の氏子代表が頭上に掲げ、厳かに歩いて進む。なお[[2005年]]（平成17年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの獅子舞百選部門）」に選定されている。 * 形状: 箱獅子 * 分類: 行道獅子

{{画像提供依頼|date=2016年12月9日|cat=射水市|祭風景}} '''やんさんま祭り'''は、[[富山県]][[射水市]][[下村]]加茂の[[加茂神社 (射水市)|下村加茂神社]]にて毎年5月4日に行われる[[流鏑馬]]行事である。3日には宵祭が5日には裏祭が行われる。
== 概要 == 京都[[加茂神社]]から勧請された下村加茂神社創建当時から始められたと云われており、「やんさんま」とは流鏑馬が訛ったもので、[[京都]][[上賀茂神社]]で行われている[[競馬会神事]]より伝わったものといわれる。また、'''牛乗式（牛つぶし）'''という全国でもここだけの神事が行われており、[[1967年]]（[[昭和]]42年）3月25日に富山県の[[無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。なお[[2006年]]（[[平成]]18年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの祭り百選部門）」に選定されている。
== 主な日程 == 3日の宵祭りには、宵祭走馬の儀、宵祭の祭式、宵祭九遍式、宵祭流鏑馬の儀の順に行われ、[[獅子舞]]の舞始祭も行われていたが、現在は宵祭走馬の儀と宵祭流鏑馬の儀が略式で行われている。
4日の本祭は、朝8時より悪魔祓いの獅子舞の舞いから始まり、神馬・走馬の儀、御戸開きの儀、神幸式、神馬式、御旅所の儀、牛乗式と続く。その後も還御、例祭式、九遍式と続き最後に流鏑馬式が行われる。
5日の裏祭は、午後2時頃より舞納祭（獅子舞）、例祭成就報賽祭が執行され、すべての諸行事が終了となる。
== 牛乗式（牛つぶし） == 鞍を載せた1頭の牡牛に、[[兜]]に[[甲冑]]を纏って赤面の大鼻の面を被り、王鼻（田の神）となった若者が乗る。神の依代は、兜の中天に挿した和紙でできた牡丹の花である。手には1[[丈]]3[[尺]]（約3.94メートル）の弓を持ち、背中には矢を背負って境内に入ると、「天下泰平」「五穀成就」を祈願して拝殿の屋根（北方）に向け、一矢を放つ。その後神幸御順路へ入ると境内の御旅所付近を3周し、次いで多くの若衆が牛に駆け寄って押さえつける。牛は嫌がって暴れるが、多くの若衆が交代で押えつけるため、やがて疲労して地面に押し付けられ、座り込まされてしまうというものである。この神事は田の神が乗った牛を座らせて留め置き、この地の五穀豊穣をいつまでも願うと共に、雨乞い行事も併せて行われているとも伝えられている。
== 流鏑馬式 == 3人の馬乗役は、紅白の[[御幣]]を中天に付けた荒目に編んだ龍笠に白紙の[[紙垂]]を着け、紫[[縮緬]]の鉢巻、緋羅紗の[[襦袢]]に白縮緬で襷掛けをし、黒[[ベルベット|天鵞絨（ベルベット）]]の丸帯に乗馬袴、手には黒の手覆いに足先には白足袋姿で馬にまたがる。
3人の馬乗役は拝殿西側から次々と杉並木の参道を駆け抜け、参道内の杉の木に設けられた高さ約5メートルにある大的に向け、それぞれ3回ずつ矢を射る。牛乗式及び流鏑馬式で放たれた矢は観客たちが奪い合うように拾い、五穀豊穣や家内繁栄などを祈って「除魔招福」の霊符として家に持ち帰り、神棚に供える。
== 獅子舞 == 獅子舞は小さな獅子頭を[[紋付]]・[[袴]]姿の氏子代表が頭上に掲げ、厳かに歩いて進むものである。なお[[2005年]]（平成17年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの獅子舞百選部門）」に選定されている。 * 形状: 箱獅子 * 分類: 行道獅子
== 関連項目 == * [[加茂神社 (射水市)|下村加茂神社]] * [[流鏑馬]] * [[無形民俗文化財]] * [[日本の祭一覧]] * [[下村]]
== 参考文献 == * 『祭礼事典・富山県』（富山県祭礼研究会 編・[[おうふう|桜楓社]]）[[1991年]]（平成3年）12月25日発行 ISBN 4-273-02481-0 * 『祭礼行事・富山県』（高橋秀雄・漆間元三 編・桜楓社）1991年（平成3年）10月25日発行 ISBN 4-273-02482-9 * 『北陸の祭り』（企画 [[北陸電力]]・チューエツ メディア制作室出版部）[[2001年]]（平成13年）3月23日発行 ISBN 4-9980730-2-8 * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』（富山県[[教育委員会]] 生涯学習・文化財室）[[2007年]]（平成19年）3月発行 * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(2) とやまの獅子舞』（富山県教育委員会 生涯学習・文化財室）[[2006年]]（平成18年）3月発行 * 『射水のまつり』（[[射水市新湊博物館]]）[[2008年]]（平成20年）10月10日発行
== 外部リンク == * [http://www4.tkc.pref.toyama.jp/video/ とやまデジタル映像ライブラリー]（紹介動画あり）
{{event-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やんさんままつり}} [[Category:富山県の祭り]] [[Category:富山県の文化]] [[Category:射水市]]

== 下村馬事公園 == 下村加茂神社に隣接して[[1986年]]（昭和61年）に、祭礼で行われる流鏑馬用の馬の飼育と練習場として整備された。また放牧場が公園から約200m離れた場所にある<ref>『とやまの公園 空中散歩 下村馬事公園』[[北日本新聞]] 2018年4月16日7面</ref>。

{{画像提供依頼|date=2016年12月9日|cat=射水市|祭風景}} '''やんさんま祭り'''は、[[富山県]][[射水市]][[下村]]加茂の[[加茂神社 (射水市)|下村加茂神社]]にて毎年5月4日に行われる[[流鏑馬]]行事である。3日には宵祭が5日には裏祭が行われる。
== 概要 == 京都[[賀茂神社]]から勧請された下村加茂神社創建当時から始められたと云われており、「やんさんま」とは流鏑馬が訛ったもので、[[京都]][[上賀茂神社]]で行われている[[競馬会神事]]より伝わったものといわれる。また、'''牛乗式（牛つぶし）'''という全国でもここだけの神事が行われており、[[1967年]]（[[昭和]]42年）3月25日に富山県の[[無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。なお[[2006年]]（[[平成]]18年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの祭り百選部門）」に選定されている。
== 主な日程 == 3日の宵祭りには、宵祭走馬の儀、宵祭の祭式、宵祭九遍式、宵祭流鏑馬の儀の順に行われ、[[獅子舞]]の舞始祭も行われていたが、現在は宵祭走馬の儀と宵祭流鏑馬の儀が略式で行われている。
4日の本祭は、朝8時より悪魔祓いの獅子舞の舞いから始まり、神馬・走馬の儀、御戸開きの儀、神幸式、神馬式、御旅所の儀、牛乗式と続く。その後も還御、例祭式、九遍式と続き最後に流鏑馬式が行われる。
5日の裏祭は、午後2時頃より舞納祭（獅子舞）、例祭成就報賽祭が執行され、すべての諸行事が終了となる。
== 牛乗式（牛つぶし） == 鞍を載せた1頭の牡牛に、[[兜]]に[[甲冑]]を纏って赤面の大鼻の面を被り、王鼻（田の神）となった若者が乗る。神の依代は、兜の中天に挿した和紙でできた牡丹の花である。手には1[[丈]]3[[尺]]（約3.94メートル）の弓を持ち、背中には矢を背負って境内に入ると、「天下泰平」「五穀成就」を祈願して拝殿の屋根（北方）に向け、一矢を放つ。その後神幸御順路へ入ると境内の御旅所付近を3周し、次いで多くの若衆が牛に駆け寄って押さえつける。牛は嫌がって暴れるが、多くの若衆が交代で押えつけるため、やがて疲労して地面に押し付けられ、座り込まされてしまうというものである。この神事は田の神が乗った牛を座らせて留め置き、この地の五穀豊穣をいつまでも願うと共に、雨乞い行事も併せて行われているとも伝えられている。
== 流鏑馬式 == 3人の馬乗役は、紅白の[[御幣]]を中天に付けた荒目に編んだ龍笠に白紙の[[紙垂]]を着け、紫[[縮緬]]の鉢巻、緋羅紗の[[襦袢]]に白縮緬で襷掛けをし、黒[[ベルベット|天鵞絨（ベルベット）]]の丸帯に乗馬袴、手には黒の手覆いに足先には白足袋姿で馬にまたがる。
3人の馬乗役は拝殿西側から次々と杉並木の参道を駆け抜け、参道内の杉の木に設けられた高さ約5メートルにある大的に向け、それぞれ3回ずつ矢を射る。牛乗式及び流鏑馬式で放たれた矢は観客たちが奪い合うように拾い、五穀豊穣や家内繁栄などを祈って「除魔招福」の霊符として家に持ち帰り、神棚に供える。
== 獅子舞 == 獅子舞は小さな獅子頭を[[紋付]]・[[袴]]姿の氏子代表が頭上に掲げ、厳かに歩いて進むものである。なお[[2005年]]（平成17年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの獅子舞百選部門）」に選定されている。 * 形状: 箱獅子 * 分類: 行道獅子
== 下村馬事公園 == 下村加茂神社に隣接して[[1986年]]（昭和61年）に、祭礼で行われる流鏑馬用の馬の飼育と練習場として整備された。また放牧場が公園から約200m離れた場所にある<ref>『とやまの公園 空中散歩 下村馬事公園』[[北日本新聞]] 2018年4月16日7面</ref>。
== 関連項目 == * [[加茂神社 (射水市)|下村加茂神社]] * [[流鏑馬]] * [[無形民俗文化財]] * [[日本の祭一覧]] * [[下村]]
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『祭礼事典・富山県』（富山県祭礼研究会 編・[[おうふう|桜楓社]]）[[1991年]]（平成3年）12月25日発行 ISBN 4-273-02481-0 * 『祭礼行事・富山県』（高橋秀雄・漆間元三 編・桜楓社）1991年（平成3年）10月25日発行 ISBN 4-273-02482-9 * 『北陸の祭り』（企画 [[北陸電力]]・チューエツ メディア制作室出版部）[[2001年]]（平成13年）3月23日発行 ISBN 4-9980730-2-8 * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』（富山県[[教育委員会]] 生涯学習・文化財室）[[2007年]]（平成19年）3月発行 * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(2) とやまの獅子舞』（富山県教育委員会 生涯学習・文化財室）[[2006年]]（平成18年）3月発行 * 『射水のまつり』（[[射水市新湊博物館]]）[[2008年]]（平成20年）10月10日発行
== 外部リンク == * [http://www4.tkc.pref.toyama.jp/video/ とやまデジタル映像ライブラリー]（紹介動画あり） * [http://www.pref.toyama.jp/sections/3009/3007/digital/03-event/shishimai/shishimai100.pdf#search='%E3%81%A8%E3%82%84%E3%81%BE%E3%81%AE%E7%8D%85%E5%AD%90%E8%88%9E' 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(2) とやまの獅子舞』（富山県教育委員会 生涯学習・文化財室）] * [http://www.pref.toyama.jp/sections/3009/3007/digital/03-event/maturi/maturi.pdf#search='%E5%AF%8C%E5%B1%B1%E3%81%AE%E7%A5%AD%E3%82%8A' 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』（富山県教育委員会 生涯学習・文化財室）]
{{event-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やんさんままつり}} [[Category:富山県の祭り]] [[Category:富山県の文化]] [[Category:射水市]]

{{画像提供依頼|date=2016年12月9日|cat=射水市|祭風景}} '''やんさんま祭り'''は、[[富山県]][[射水市]][[下村]]加茂の[[加茂神社 (射水市)|下村加茂神社]]にて毎年5月4日に行われる[[流鏑馬]]行事である。3日には宵祭が5日には裏祭が行われる。
== 概要 == 京都[[賀茂神社]]から勧請された下村加茂神社創建当時から始められたと云われており、「やんさんま」とは流鏑馬が訛ったもので、[[京都]][[上賀茂神社]]で行われている[[競馬会神事]]より伝わったものといわれる。また、'''牛乗式（牛つぶし）'''という全国でもここだけの神事が行われており、[[1967年]]（[[昭和]]42年）3月25日に富山県の[[無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。なお[[2006年]]（[[平成]]18年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの祭り百選部門）」に選定されている。

{{画像提供依頼|date=2016年12月9日|cat=射水市|祭風景}} '''やんさんま祭り'''は、[[富山県]][[射水市]][[下村]]加茂の[[加茂神社 (射水市)|下村加茂神社]]にて毎年5月4日に行われる[[流鏑馬]]行事である。3日には宵祭が5日には裏祭が行われる。

== 牛乗式（牛つぶし） == 鞍を載せた1頭の牡牛に、[[兜]]に[[甲冑]]を纏って赤面の大鼻の面を被り、王鼻（田の神）となった若者が乗る儀式である。神の依代は、兜の中天に挿した和紙でできた牡丹の花である。手には1[[丈]]3[[尺]]（約3.94メートル）の弓を持ち、背中には矢を背負って境内に入ると、「天下泰平」「五穀成就」を祈願して拝殿の屋根（北方）に向け、一矢を放つ。その後神幸御順路へ入ると境内の御旅所付近を3周し、次いで多くの若衆が牛に駆け寄って押さえつける。牛は嫌がって暴れるが、多くの若衆が交代で押えつけるため、やがて疲労して地面に押し付けられ、座り込まされてしまうというものである。この神事は田の神が乗った牛を座らせて留め置き、この地の五穀豊穣をいつまでも願うと共に、雨乞い行事も併せて行われているとも伝えられている。
== 流鏑馬式 == 3人の馬乗役は、紅白の[[御幣]]を中天に付けた荒目に編んだ龍笠に白紙の[[紙垂]]を着け、紫[[縮緬]]の鉢巻、緋羅紗の[[襦袢]]に白縮緬で襷掛けをし、黒[[ベルベット|天鵞絨（ベルベット）]]の丸帯に乗馬袴、手には黒の手覆いに足先には白足袋姿で馬にまたがる。
3人の馬乗役は拝殿西側から次々と杉並木の参道を駆け抜け、参道内の杉の木に設けられた高さ約5メートルにある大的に向け、それぞれ3回ずつ矢を射る。牛乗式及び流鏑馬式で放たれた矢は観客たちが奪い合うように拾い、五穀豊穣や家内繁栄などを祈って「除魔招福」の霊符として家に持ち帰り、神棚に供える。
== 獅子舞 == 獅子舞は小さな獅子頭を[[紋付]]・[[袴]]姿の氏子代表が頭上に掲げ、厳かに歩いて進むものである。なお[[2005年]]（平成17年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの獅子舞百選部門）」に選定されている。 * 形状: 箱獅子 * 分類: 行道獅子
== 下村馬事公園 == 下村加茂神社に隣接して[[1986年]]（昭和61年）に、祭礼で行われる流鏑馬用の馬の飼育と練習場として整備された。また放牧場が公園から約200m離れた場所にある<ref>『とやまの公園 空中散歩 下村馬事公園』北日本新聞 2018年4月16日7面</ref>。
== 関連項目 == * [[加茂神社 (射水市)|下村加茂神社]] * [[流鏑馬]] * [[無形民俗文化財]] * [[日本の祭一覧]] * [[下村]]
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}

{{画像提供依頼|date=2016年12月9日|cat=射水市|祭風景}} '''やんさんま祭り'''は、[[富山県]][[射水市]][[下村]]加茂の[[加茂神社 (射水市)|下村加茂神社]]にて毎年5月4日に行われる[[流鏑馬]]行事である。3日には宵祭が5日には裏祭が行われる。
== 概要 == 京都[[賀茂神社]]から勧請された下村加茂神社創建当時から始められたと云われており、「やんさんま」とは流鏑馬が訛ったもので、[[京都]][[上賀茂神社]]で行われている[[競馬会神事]]より伝わったものといわれる。また、'''牛乗式（牛つぶし）'''という全国でもここだけの神事が行われており、[[1967年]]（[[昭和]]42年）3月25日に富山県の[[無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。なお[[2006年]]（[[平成]]18年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの祭り百選部門）」に選定されている。
[[2020年]]（[[令和]]2年）4月4日、[[新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019年-)|新型コロナウイルス感染症]]の拡大を受け、関係諸団体はこの年の主な神事の中止<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">『射水下村加茂神社 やんさんま祭り 主要神事中止』[[北日本新聞]] 2020年4月5日25面</ref>。[[2021年]]（令和3年）も、拝殿での神事を除き主な神事は中止となる<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">『やんさんま 主要神事中止』北日本新聞 2021年4月7日21面</ref>。
== 主な日程 == 3日の宵祭りには、宵祭走馬の儀、宵祭の祭式、宵祭九遍式、宵祭流鏑馬の儀の順に行われ、[[獅子舞]]の舞始祭も行われていたが、現在は宵祭走馬の儀と宵祭流鏑馬の儀が略式で行われている。
4日の本祭は、朝8時より悪魔祓いの獅子舞の舞いから始まり、神馬・走馬の儀、御戸開きの儀、神幸式、神馬式、御旅所の儀、牛乗式と続く。その後も還御、例祭式、九遍式と続き最後に流鏑馬式が行われる。
5日の裏祭は、午後2時頃より舞納祭（獅子舞）、例祭成就報賽祭が執行され、すべての諸行事が終了となる。
== 牛乗式（牛つぶし） == 鞍を載せた1頭の牡牛に、[[兜]]に[[甲冑]]を纏って赤面の大鼻の面を被り、王鼻（田の神）となった若者が乗る儀式である。神の依代は、兜の中天に挿した和紙でできた牡丹の花である。手には1[[丈]]3[[尺]]（約3.94メートル）の弓を持ち、背中には矢を背負って境内に入ると、「天下泰平」「五穀成就」を祈願して拝殿の屋根（北方）に向け、一矢を放つ。その後神幸御順路へ入ると境内の御旅所付近を3周し、次いで多くの若衆が牛に駆け寄って押さえつける。牛は嫌がって暴れるが、多くの若衆が交代で押えつけるため、やがて疲労して地面に押し付けられ、座り込まされてしまうというものである。この神事は田の神が乗った牛を座らせて留め置き、この地の五穀豊穣をいつまでも願うと共に、雨乞い行事も併せて行われているとも伝えられている。
== 流鏑馬式 == 3人の馬乗役は、紅白の[[御幣]]を中天に付けた荒目に編んだ龍笠に白紙の[[紙垂]]を着け、紫[[縮緬]]の鉢巻、緋羅紗の[[襦袢]]に白縮緬で襷掛けをし、黒[[ベルベット|天鵞絨（ベルベット）]]の丸帯に乗馬袴、手には黒の手覆いに足先には白足袋姿で馬にまたがる。
3人の馬乗役は拝殿西側から次々と杉並木の参道を駆け抜け、参道内の杉の木に設けられた高さ約5メートルにある大的に向け、それぞれ3回ずつ矢を射る。牛乗式及び流鏑馬式で放たれた矢は観客たちが奪い合うように拾い、五穀豊穣や家内繁栄などを祈って「除魔招福」の霊符として家に持ち帰り、神棚に供える。
== 獅子舞 == 獅子舞は小さな獅子頭を[[紋付]]・[[袴]]姿の氏子代表が頭上に掲げ、厳かに歩いて進むものである。なお[[2005年]]（平成17年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの獅子舞百選部門）」に選定されている。 * 形状: 箱獅子 * 分類: 行道獅子
== 下村馬事公園 == 下村加茂神社に隣接して[[1986年]]（昭和61年）に、祭礼で行われる流鏑馬用の馬の飼育と練習場として整備された。また放牧場が公園から約200m離れた場所にある<ref>『とやまの公園 空中散歩 下村馬事公園』北日本新聞 2018年4月16日7面</ref>。

{{画像提供依頼|date=2016年12月9日|cat=射水市|祭風景}} '''やんさんま祭り'''は、[[富山県]][[射水市]]加茂中部の[[加茂神社 (射水市)|下村加茂神社]]にて毎年5月4日に行われる[[流鏑馬]]行事である。3日には宵祭が5日には裏祭が行われる。
== 概要 == 京都[[賀茂神社]]から勧請された下村加茂神社創建当時から始められたと云われており、「やんさんま」とは流鏑馬が訛ったもので、[[京都]][[上賀茂神社]]で行われている[[競馬会神事]]より伝わったものといわれる。また、'''牛乗式（牛つぶし）'''という全国でもここだけの神事が行われており、[[1967年]]（[[昭和]]42年）3月25日に富山県の[[無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。なお[[2006年]]（[[平成]]18年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの祭り百選部門）」に選定されている。
[[2020年]]（[[令和]]2年）4月4日、[[新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019年-)|新型コロナウイルス感染症]]の拡大を受け、関係諸団体はこの年の主な神事の中止<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">『射水下村加茂神社 やんさんま祭り 主要神事中止』[[北日本新聞]] 2020年4月5日25面</ref>。[[2021年]]（令和3年）も、拝殿での神事を除き主な神事は中止となる<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">『やんさんま 主要神事中止』北日本新聞 2021年4月7日21面</ref>。
== 主な日程 == 3日の宵祭りには、宵祭走馬の儀、宵祭の祭式、宵祭九遍式、宵祭流鏑馬の儀の順に行われ、[[獅子舞]]の舞始祭も行われていたが、現在は宵祭走馬の儀と宵祭流鏑馬の儀が略式で行われている。
4日の本祭は、朝8時より悪魔祓いの獅子舞の舞いから始まり、神馬・走馬の儀、御戸開きの儀、神幸式、神馬式、御旅所の儀、牛乗式と続く。その後も還御、例祭式、九遍式と続き最後に流鏑馬式が行われる。
5日の裏祭は、午後2時頃より舞納祭（獅子舞）、例祭成就報賽祭が執行され、すべての諸行事が終了となる。
== 牛乗式（牛つぶし） == 鞍を載せた1頭の牡牛に、[[兜]]に[[甲冑]]を纏って赤面の大鼻の面を被り、王鼻（田の神）となった若者が乗る儀式である。神の依代は、兜の中天に挿した和紙でできた牡丹の花である。手には1[[丈]]3[[尺]]（約3.94メートル）の弓を持ち、背中には矢を背負って境内に入ると、「天下泰平」「五穀成就」を祈願して拝殿の屋根（北方）に向け、一矢を放つ。その後神幸御順路へ入ると境内の御旅所付近を3周し、次いで多くの若衆が牛に駆け寄って押さえつける。牛は嫌がって暴れるが、多くの若衆が交代で押えつけるため、やがて疲労して地面に押し付けられ、座り込まされてしまうというものである。この神事は田の神が乗った牛を座らせて留め置き、この地の五穀豊穣をいつまでも願うと共に、雨乞い行事も併せて行われているとも伝えられている。
== 流鏑馬式 == 3人の馬乗役は、紅白の[[御幣]]を中天に付けた荒目に編んだ龍笠に白紙の[[紙垂]]を着け、紫[[縮緬]]の鉢巻、緋羅紗の[[襦袢]]に白縮緬で襷掛けをし、黒[[ベルベット|天鵞絨（ベルベット）]]の丸帯に乗馬袴、手には黒の手覆いに足先には白足袋姿で馬にまたがる。
3人の馬乗役は拝殿西側から次々と杉並木の参道を駆け抜け、参道内の杉の木に設けられた高さ約5メートルにある大的に向け、それぞれ3回ずつ矢を射る。牛乗式及び流鏑馬式で放たれた矢は観客たちが奪い合うように拾い、五穀豊穣や家内繁栄などを祈って「除魔招福」の霊符として家に持ち帰り、神棚に供える。
== 獅子舞 == 獅子舞は小さな獅子頭を[[紋付]]・[[袴]]姿の氏子代表が頭上に掲げ、厳かに歩いて進むものである。なお[[2005年]]（平成17年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの獅子舞百選部門）」に選定されている。 * 形状: 箱獅子 * 分類: 行道獅子
== 下村馬事公園 == 下村加茂神社に隣接して[[1986年]]（昭和61年）に、祭礼で行われる流鏑馬用の馬の飼育と練習場として整備された。また放牧場が公園から約200m離れた場所にある<ref>『とやまの公園 空中散歩 下村馬事公園』北日本新聞 2018年4月16日7面</ref>。
== 関連項目 == * [[加茂神社 (射水市)|下村加茂神社]] * [[流鏑馬]] * [[無形民俗文化財]] * [[日本の祭一覧]]
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『祭礼事典・富山県』（富山県祭礼研究会 編・[[おうふう|桜楓社]]）[[1991年]]（平成3年）12月25日発行 ISBN 4-273-02481-0 * 『祭礼行事・富山県』（高橋秀雄・漆間元三 編・桜楓社）1991年（平成3年）10月25日発行 ISBN 4-273-02482-9 * 『北陸の祭り』（企画 [[北陸電力]]・チューエツ メディア制作室出版部）[[2001年]]（平成13年）3月23日発行 ISBN 4-9980730-2-8 * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』（富山県[[教育委員会]] 生涯学習・文化財室）[[2007年]]（平成19年）3月発行 * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(2) とやまの獅子舞』（富山県教育委員会 生涯学習・文化財室）[[2006年]]（平成18年）3月発行 * 『射水のまつり』（[[射水市新湊博物館]]）[[2008年]]（平成20年）10月10日発行
== 外部リンク == * [http://www4.tkc.pref.toyama.jp/video/ とやまデジタル映像ライブラリー]（紹介動画あり） * [http://www.pref.toyama.jp/sections/3009/3007/digital/03-event/shishimai/shishimai100.pdf#search='%E3%81%A8%E3%82%84%E3%81%BE%E3%81%AE%E7%8D%85%E5%AD%90%E8%88%9E' 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(2) とやまの獅子舞』（富山県教育委員会 生涯学習・文化財室）] * [http://www.pref.toyama.jp/sections/3009/3007/digital/03-event/maturi/maturi.pdf#search='%E5%AF%8C%E5%B1%B1%E3%81%AE%E7%A5%AD%E3%82%8A' 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』（富山県教育委員会 生涯学習・文化財室）]

revision.text:
'''Yansanma Festival''' (やんさんま祭り) is a [[yabusame]] event held annually on May 4 at the {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} in {{ill|Imizu City|ja|射水市}}, Toyama Prefecture. A pre-festival (宵祭) takes place on May 3, and a post-festival (裏祭) is held on May 5.
== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} consecrated from {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|賀茂神社}}. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社}} in Kyoto.
One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019年-)}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion, wrapping up all events by around 2:00 PM.
== Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony) == In this ritual, a young man dressed as "Ōhana" (田の神, deity of rice fields) wears red-faced and large-nosed masks and full armor, including a helmet adorned with a paper peony flower symbolizing the deity. He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 [[ken]] and 3 [[shaku]] (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref>
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} * {{ill|Yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} * {{ill|List of Japanese Festivals|ja|日本の祭一覧}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Festivals of Toyama Prefecture, Toyama Prefecture Festival Research Association, Sakuraōfu-sha, December 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02481-0}} * Traditional Events of Toyama Prefecture, Hidehiro Takahashi and Motomi Urushima, Sakuraōfu-sha, October 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02482-9}} * Festivals of Hokuriku, Hokuriku Electric Power Company Planning and Chūetsu Media Production Division Publishing Department, March 23, 2001, {{ISBN2|4-9980730-2-8}} * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (3): Festivals of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2007 * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (2): Lion Dances of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2006 * Festivals of Imizu, Imizu City Shinminato Museum, October 10, 2008
== External Links == * [https://www.shimomura-kamojinja.com/post_project/0504-2 Kamo Festival (May 4) | Shimomura Kamo Shrine Official Website]
{{event-stub}} [[Category:Festivals in Toyama Prefecture]] [[Category:Imizu City]] [[ja:やんさんま祭り]]

On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019年-)}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>

The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019年-)}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion, wrapping up all events by around 2:00 PM.
== Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony) == In this ritual, a young man dressed as "Ōhana" (田の神, deity of rice fields) wears red-faced and large-nosed masks and full armor, including a helmet adorned with a paper peony flower symbolizing the deity. He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 [[ken]] and 3 [[shaku]] (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref>

'''Yansanma Festival''' (やんさんま祭り) is a [[yabusame]] event held annually on May 4 at the {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} in {{ill|Imizu City|ja|射水市}}, Toyama Prefecture. A pre-festival (宵祭) takes place on May 3, and a post-festival (裏祭) is held on May 5.
== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} consecrated from {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|賀茂神社}}. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社}} in Kyoto.
One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019年-)}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion, wrapping up all events by around 2:00 PM.
== Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony) == In this ritual, a young man dressed as "Ōhana" (田の神, deity of rice fields) wears red-faced and large-nosed masks and full armor, including a helmet adorned with a paper peony flower symbolizing the deity. He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 [[ken]] and 3 [[shaku]] (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref>
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} * {{ill|Yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} * {{ill|List of Japanese Festivals|ja|日本の祭一覧}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Festivals of Toyama Prefecture, Toyama Prefecture Festival Research Association, Sakuraōfu-sha, December 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02481-0}} * Traditional Events of Toyama Prefecture, Hidehiro Takahashi and Motomi Urushima, Sakuraōfu-sha, October 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02482-9}} * Festivals of Hokuriku, Hokuriku Electric Power Company Planning and Chūetsu Media Production Division Publishing Department, March 23, 2001, {{ISBN2|4-9980730-2-8}} * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (3): Festivals of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2007 * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (2): Lion Dances of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2006 * Festivals of Imizu, Imizu City Shinminato Museum, October 10, 2008
== External Links == * [https://www.shimomura-kamojinja.com/post_project/0504-2 Kamo Festival (May 4) | Shimomura Kamo Shrine Official Website]

'''Yansanma Festival''' (やんさんま祭り) is a [[yabusame]] event held annually on May 4 at the {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} in {{ill|Imizu City|ja|射水市}}, Toyama Prefecture. A pre-festival (宵祭) takes place on May 3, and a post-festival (裏祭) is held on May 5.
== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} consecrated from {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|賀茂神社}}. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社}} in Kyoto.
One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019年-)}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion, wrapping up all events by around 2:00 PM.
== Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony) == In this ritual, a young man dressed as "Ōhana" (田の神, deity of rice fields) wears red-faced and large-nosed masks and full armor, including a helmet adorned with a paper peony flower symbolizing the deity. He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 [[ken]] and 3 [[shaku]] (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref>
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} * {{ill|Yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} * {{ill|List of Japanese Festivals|ja|日本の祭一覧}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Festivals of Toyama Prefecture, Toyama Prefecture Festival Research Association, Sakuraōfu-sha, December 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02481-0}} * Traditional Events of Toyama Prefecture, Hidehiro Takahashi and Motomi Urushima, Sakuraōfu-sha, October 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02482-9}} * Festivals of Hokuriku, Hokuriku Electric Power Company Planning and Chūetsu Media Production Division Publishing Department, March 23, 2001, {{ISBN2|4-9980730-2-8}} * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (3): Festivals of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2007 * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (2): Lion Dances of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2006 * Festivals of Imizu, Imizu City Shinminato Museum, October 10, 2008
== External Links == * [https://www.shimomura-kamojinja.com/post_project/0504-2 Kamo Festival (May 4) | Shimomura Kamo Shrine Official Website]

== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} consecrated from {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Kyoto)|ja|賀茂神社}}. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社}} in Kyoto.
One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019年-)}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion, wrapping up all events by around 2:00 PM.
== Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony) == In this ritual, a young man dressed as "Ōhana" (田の神, deity of rice fields) wears red-faced and large-nosed masks and full armor, including a helmet adorned with a paper peony flower symbolizing the deity. He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 [[ken]] and 3 [[shaku]] (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref>
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} * {{ill|Yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} * {{ill|List of Japanese Festivals|ja|日本の祭一覧}}

On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019年-)}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion, wrapping up all events by around 2:00 PM.
== Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony) == In this ritual, a young man dressed as "Ōhana" (田の神, deity of rice fields) wears red-faced and large-nosed masks and full armor, including a helmet adorned with a paper peony flower symbolizing the deity. He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 [[ken]] and 3 [[shaku]] (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref>
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} * {{ill|Yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} * {{ill|List of Japanese Festivals|ja|日本の祭一覧}}

'''Yansanma Festival''' (やんさんま祭り) is a [[yabusame]] event held annually on May 4 at the {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} in {{ill|Imizu City|ja|射水市}}, Toyama Prefecture. A pre-festival (宵祭) takes place on May 3, and a post-festival (裏祭) is held on May 5.
== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} consecrated from {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|賀茂神社}}. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社}} in Kyoto.

The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.

The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.

They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. revision.text:
'''Yansanma Festival''' (やんさんま祭り) is a [[yabusame]] event held annually on May 4 at the {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} in {{ill|Imizu City|ja|射水市}}, Toyama Prefecture. A pre-festival (宵祭) takes place on May 3, and a post-festival (裏祭) is held on May 5.
== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} consecrated from {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|賀茂神社}}. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社}} in Kyoto.
One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019年-)}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion, wrapping up all events by around 2:00 PM.
== Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony) == In this ritual, a young man dressed as "Ōhana" (田の神, deity of rice fields) wears red-faced and large-nosed masks and full armor, including a helmet adorned with a paper peony flower symbolizing the deity. He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 [[ken]] and 3 [[shaku]] (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref>
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} * {{ill|Yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} * {{ill|List of Japanese Festivals|ja|日本の祭一覧}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Festivals of Toyama Prefecture, Toyama Prefecture Festival Research Association, Sakuraōfu-sha, December 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02481-0}} * Traditional Events of Toyama Prefecture, Hidehiro Takahashi and Motomi Urushima, Sakuraōfu-sha, October 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02482-9}} * Festivals of Hokuriku, Hokuriku Electric Power Company Planning and Chūetsu Media Production Division Publishing Department, March 23, 2001, {{ISBN2|4-9980730-2-8}} * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (3): Festivals of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2007 * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (2): Lion Dances of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2006 * Festivals of Imizu, Imizu City Shinminato Museum, October 10, 2008
== External Links == * [https://www.shimomura-kamojinja.com/post_project/0504-2 Kamo Festival (May 4) | Shimomura Kamo Shrine Official Website]

revision.text:
'''Yansanma Festival''' (やんさんま祭り) is a [[yabusame]] event held annually on May 4 at the {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} in {{ill|Imizu City|ja|射水市}}, Toyama Prefecture. A pre-festival (宵祭) takes place on May 3, and a post-festival (裏祭) is held on May 5.
== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} consecrated from {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|賀茂神社}}. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社}} in Kyoto.
One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019年-)}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion, wrapping up all events by around 2:00 PM.
== Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony) == In this ritual, a young man dressed as "Ōhana" (田の神, deity of rice fields) wears red-faced and large-nosed masks and full armor, including a helmet adorned with a paper peony flower symbolizing the deity. He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 [[ken]] and 3 [[shaku]] (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref>
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} * {{ill|Yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} * {{ill|List of Japanese Festivals|ja|日本の祭一覧}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Festivals of Toyama Prefecture, Toyama Prefecture Festival Research Association, Sakuraōfu-sha, December 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02481-0}} * Traditional Events of Toyama Prefecture, Hidehiro Takahashi and Motomi Urushima, Sakuraōfu-sha, October 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02482-9}} * Festivals of Hokuriku, Hokuriku Electric Power Company Planning and Chūetsu Media Production Division Publishing Department, March 23, 2001, {{ISBN2|4-9980730-2-8}} * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (3): Festivals of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2007 * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (2): Lion Dances of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2006 * Festivals of Imizu, Imizu City Shinminato Museum, October 10, 2008
== External Links == * [https://www.shimomura-kamojinja.com/post_project/0504-2 Kamo Festival (May 4) | Shimomura Kamo Shrine Official Website]

[[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yansanma Festival}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Yansanma Festival-qq]] [[Category:Yansanma Festival]][[Category:Imizu City]] [[Category:Yansanma Festival]][[Category:Yansanma Festival]][[Category:Yansanma Festival]][[Category:dd]][[Category:Yansanma Festival]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yansanma Festival]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[ja:やんさんま祭り]]

'''Yansanma Festival''' (やんさんま祭り) is a [[yabusame]] event held annually on May 4 at the {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} in {{ill|Imizu City|ja|射水市}}, Toyama Prefecture. A pre-festival (宵祭) takes place on May 3, and a post-festival (裏祭) is held on May 5.
== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} consecrated from {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|賀茂神社}}. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社}} in Kyoto.
One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019年-)}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion, wrapping up all events by around 2:00 PM.
== Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony) == In this ritual, a young man dressed as "Ōhana" (田の神, deity of rice fields) wears red-faced and large-nosed masks and full armor, including a helmet adorned with a paper peony flower symbolizing the deity. He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 [[ken]] and 3 [[shaku]] (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref>
== See Also == * {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} * {{ill|Yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} * {{ill|List of Japanese Festivals|ja|日本の祭一覧}}

revision.text:
'''Yansanma Festival''' (やんさんま祭り) is a [[yabusame]] event held annually on May 4 at the {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} in {{ill|Imizu City|ja|射水市}}, Toyama Prefecture. A pre-festival (宵祭) takes place on May 3, and a post-festival (裏祭) is held on May 5.
== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} consecrated from {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|賀茂神社}}. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社}} in Kyoto.
One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019年-)}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion, wrapping up all events by around 2:00 PM.
== Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony) == In this ritual, a young man dressed as "Ōhana" (田の神, deity of rice fields) wears red-faced and large-nosed masks and full armor, including a helmet adorned with a paper peony flower symbolizing the deity. He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 [[ken]] and 3 [[shaku]] (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref>
== See Also == * {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} * {{ill|Yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} * {{ill|List of Japanese Festivals|ja|日本の祭一覧}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Festivals of Toyama Prefecture, Toyama Prefecture Festival Research Association, Sakuraōfu-sha, December 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02481-0}} * Traditional Events of Toyama Prefecture, Hidehiro Takahashi and Motomi Urushima, Sakuraōfu-sha, October 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02482-9}} * Festivals of Hokuriku, Hokuriku Electric Power Company Planning and Chūetsu Media Production Division Publishing Department, March 23, 2001, {{ISBN2|4-9980730-2-8}} * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (3): Festivals of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2007 * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (2): Lion Dances of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2006 * Festivals of Imizu, Imizu City Shinminato Museum, October 10, 2008
== External Links == * [https://www.shimomura-kamojinja.com/post_project/0504-2 Kamo Festival (May 4) | Shimomura Kamo Shrine Official Website]

'''Yansanma Festival''' (やんさんま祭り) is a [[yabusame]] event held annually on May 4 at the {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} in {{ill|Imizu City|ja|射水市}}, Toyama Prefecture. A pre-festival (宵祭) takes place on May 3, and a post-festival (裏祭) is held on May 5.
== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} consecrated from {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|賀茂神社}}. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社}} in Kyoto.
One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019年-)}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion, wrapping up all events by around 2:00 PM.
== Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony) == In this ritual, a young man dressed as "Ōhana" (田の神, deity of rice fields) wears red-faced and large-nosed masks and full armor, including a helmet adorned with a paper peony flower symbolizing the deity. He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 [[ken]] and 3 [[shaku]] (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref>
== See Also == * {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} * {{ill|Yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} * {{ill|List of Japanese Festivals|ja|日本の祭一覧}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Festivals of Toyama Prefecture, Toyama Prefecture Festival Research Association, Sakuraōfu-sha, December 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02481-0}} * Traditional Events of Toyama Prefecture, Hidehiro Takahashi and Motomi Urushima, Sakuraōfu-sha, October 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02482-9}} * Festivals of Hokuriku, Hokuriku Electric Power Company Planning and Chūetsu Media Production Division Publishing Department, March 23, 2001, {{ISBN2|4-9980730-2-8}} * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (3): Festivals of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2007 * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (2): Lion Dances of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2006 * Festivals of Imizu, Imizu City Shinminato Museum, October 10, 2008
== External Links == * [https://www.shimomura-kamojinja.com/post_project/0504-2 Kamo Festival (May 4) | Shimomura Kamo Shrine Official Website]

'''Yansanma Festival''' (やんさんま祭り) is a [[yabusame]] event held annually on May 4 at the {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} in {{ill|Imizu City|ja|射水市}}, Toyama Prefecture. A pre-festival (宵祭) takes place on May 3, and a post-festival (裏祭) is held on May 5.
== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} consecrated from {{ill|Kamo shrines|ja|賀茂神社}}. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社}} in Kyoto.
One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019年-)}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion, wrapping up all events by around 2:00 PM.
== Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony) == In this ritual, a young man dressed as "Ōhana" (田の神, deity of rice fields) wears red-faced and large-nosed masks and full armor, including a helmet adorned with a paper peony flower symbolizing the deity. He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 [[ken]] and 3 [[shaku]] (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref>
== See Also == * {{ill|Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)}} * {{ill|Yabusame|ja|流鏑馬}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} * {{ill|List of Japanese Festivals|ja|日本の祭一覧}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Festivals of Toyama Prefecture, Toyama Prefecture Festival Research Association, Sakuraōfu-sha, December 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02481-0}} * Traditional Events of Toyama Prefecture, Hidehiro Takahashi and Motomi Urushima, Sakuraōfu-sha, October 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02482-9}} * Festivals of Hokuriku, Hokuriku Electric Power Company Planning and Chūetsu Media Production Division Publishing Department, March 23, 2001, {{ISBN2|4-9980730-2-8}} * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (3): Festivals of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2007 * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (2): Lion Dances of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2006 * Festivals of Imizu, Imizu City Shinminato Museum, October 10, 2008
== External Links == * [https://www.shimomura-kamojinja.com/post_project/0504-2 Kamo Festival (May 4) | Shimomura Kamo Shrine Official Website]

One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine.
