revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:やんさんま祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:やんさんま祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:やんさんま祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q24887792]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Yansanma Festival''' (やんさんま祭り) is a [[yabusame]] event held annually on May 4 at the {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimomura Kamo Shrine|qq=}} in [[:en:Imizu|Imizu City]], Toyama Prefecture. A pre-festival (宵祭) takes place on May 3, and a post-festival (裏祭) is held on May 5.
== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] consecrated from [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo shrines]]. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurabeuma-shinji|1=Kurabe-uma-e-Jinji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598605}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=賀茂別雷神社}} in Kyoto.
One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019th year -)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/COVID-19 pandemic|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion tier 1]] and 3 [[:en:Shaku|Shaku]] (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimomura Kamo Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Yabusame|Yabusame]] * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} * [[:en:List of festivals in Japan|List of Japanese Festivals]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Festivals of Toyama Prefecture, Toyama Prefecture Festival Research Association, Sakuraōfu-sha, December 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02481-0}} * Traditional Events of Toyama Prefecture, Hidehiro Takahashi and Motomi Urushima, Sakuraōfu-sha, October 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02482-9}} * Festivals of Hokuriku, Hokuriku Electric Power Company Planning and Chūetsu Media Production Division Publishing Department, March 23, 2001, {{ISBN2|4-9980730-2-8}} * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (3): Festivals of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2007 * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (2): Lion Dances of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2006 * Festivals of Imizu, Imizu City Shinminato Museum, October 10, 2008
== External Links == * [https://www.shimomura-kamojinja.com/post_project/0504-2 Kamo Festival (May 4) | Shimomura Kamo Shrine Official Website]

== Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony) == In this ritual, a young man dressed as "Ōhana" (田の神, deity of rice fields) wears red-faced and large-nosed masks and full armor, including a helmet adorned with a paper peony flower symbolizing the deity. He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 [[:en:Ken|Ken]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]]

== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] consecrated from [[:en:Kamo shrines|Kamo shrines]]. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurabeuma-shinji|1=Kurabe-uma-e-Jinji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598605}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=賀茂別雷神社}} in Kyoto.
One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019th year -)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/COVID-19 pandemic|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion tier 1]] and 3 [[:en:Shaku|Shaku]] (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimomura Kamo Shrine|qq=}} * [[:en:Yabusame|Yabusame]] * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} * [[:en:List of festivals in Japan|List of Japanese Festivals]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Festivals of Toyama Prefecture, Toyama Prefecture Festival Research Association, Sakuraōfu-sha, December 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02481-0}} * Traditional Events of Toyama Prefecture, Hidehiro Takahashi and Motomi Urushima, Sakuraōfu-sha, October 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02482-9}} * Festivals of Hokuriku, Hokuriku Electric Power Company Planning and Chūetsu Media Production Division Publishing Department, March 23, 2001, {{ISBN2|4-9980730-2-8}} * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (3): Festivals of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2007 * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (2): Lion Dances of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2006 * Festivals of Imizu, Imizu City Shinminato Museum, October 10, 2008
== External Links == * [https://www.shimomura-kamojinja.com/post_project/0504-2 Kamo Festival (May 4) | Shimomura Kamo Shrine Official Website]

He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 [[:en:Ken|Ken]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Festivals in Toyama Prefecture]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Image Requests for Imizu City]] [[Category:Imizu, Toyama]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:やんさんま祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:やんさんま祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:やんさんま祭り]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q24887792]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Yansanma Festival''' (やんさんま祭り) is a [[yabusame]] event held annually on May 4 at the {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimomura Kamo Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Imizu|en|Imizu|WD=Q851411|lt=Imizu City}}, Toyama Prefecture. A pre-festival (宵祭) takes place on May 3, and a post-festival (裏祭) is held on May 5.
== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} consecrated from {{ill|Kamo shrines|en|Kamo shrines|WD=Q1306606|lt=Kamo shrines}}. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurabeuma-shinji|1=Kurabe-uma-e-Jinji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598605}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=賀茂別雷神社}} in Kyoto.
One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019th year -)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/COVID-19 pandemic|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion tier 1]] and 3 {{ill|Shaku|en|Shaku|WD=Q7487087|lt=Shaku}} (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimomura Kamo Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Yabusame|en|Yabusame|WD=Q1886157|lt=Yabusame}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|en|List of festivals in Japan|WD=Q10312376|lt=List of Japanese Festivals}}

After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimomura Kamo Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Yabusame|en|Yabusame|WD=Q1886157|lt=Yabusame}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|en|List of festivals in Japan|WD=Q10312376|lt=List of Japanese Festivals}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Festivals of Toyama Prefecture, Toyama Prefecture Festival Research Association, Sakuraōfu-sha, December 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02481-0}} * Traditional Events of Toyama Prefecture, Hidehiro Takahashi and Motomi Urushima, Sakuraōfu-sha, October 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02482-9}} * Festivals of Hokuriku, Hokuriku Electric Power Company Planning and Chūetsu Media Production Division Publishing Department, March 23, 2001, {{ISBN2|4-9980730-2-8}} * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (3): Festivals of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2007 * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (2): Lion Dances of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2006 * Festivals of Imizu, Imizu City Shinminato Museum, October 10, 2008
== External Links == * [https://www.shimomura-kamojinja.com/post_project/0504-2 Kamo Festival (May 4) | Shimomura Kamo Shrine Official Website]

== Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony) == In this ritual, a young man dressed as "Ōhana" (田の神, deity of rice fields) wears red-faced and large-nosed masks and full armor, including a helmet adorned with a paper peony flower symbolizing the deity. He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 {{ill|Ken|en|Ken|WD=Q226769|lt=Ken}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Festivals in Toyama Prefecture]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Image Requests for Imizu City]] [[Category:Imizu, Toyama]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:やんさんま祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:やんさんま祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:やんさんま祭り]]
'''Yansanma Festival''' (やんさんま祭り) is a [[yabusame]] event held annually on May 4 at the {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimomura Kamo Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Imizu|en|Imizu|WD=Q851411|lt=Imizu City}}, Toyama Prefecture. A pre-festival (宵祭) takes place on May 3, and a post-festival (裏祭) is held on May 5.
== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} consecrated from {{ill|Kamo shrines|en|Kamo shrines|WD=Q1306606|lt=Kamo shrines}}. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurabeuma-shinji|1=Kurabe-uma-e-Jinji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598605}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=賀茂別雷神社}} in Kyoto.
One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019th year -)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/COVID-19 pandemic|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion tier 1]] and 3 {{ill|Shaku|en|Shaku|WD=Q7487087|lt=Shaku}} (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimomura Kamo Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Yabusame|en|Yabusame|WD=Q1886157|lt=Yabusame}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|en|List of festivals in Japan|WD=Q10312376|lt=List of Japanese Festivals}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Festivals of Toyama Prefecture, Toyama Prefecture Festival Research Association, Sakuraōfu-sha, December 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02481-0}} * Traditional Events of Toyama Prefecture, Hidehiro Takahashi and Motomi Urushima, Sakuraōfu-sha, October 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02482-9}} * Festivals of Hokuriku, Hokuriku Electric Power Company Planning and Chūetsu Media Production Division Publishing Department, March 23, 2001, {{ISBN2|4-9980730-2-8}} * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (3): Festivals of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2007 * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (2): Lion Dances of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2006 * Festivals of Imizu, Imizu City Shinminato Museum, October 10, 2008
== External Links == * [https://www.shimomura-kamojinja.com/post_project/0504-2 Kamo Festival (May 4) | Shimomura Kamo Shrine Official Website]

== Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony) == In this ritual, a young man dressed as "Ōhana" (田の神, deity of rice fields) wears red-faced and large-nosed masks and full armor, including a helmet adorned with a paper peony flower symbolizing the deity. He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 {{ill|Ken|en|Ken|WD=Q226769|lt=Ken}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Festivals in Toyama Prefecture]] [[Category:Image Requests for Imizu City]] [[Category:Imizu, Toyama]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:やんさんま祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:やんさんま祭り]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:やんさんま祭り]]
'''Yansanma Festival''' (やんさんま祭り) is a [[yabusame]] event held annually on May 4 at the {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimomura Kamo Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Imizu|en|Imizu|WD=Q851411|lt=Imizu City}}, Toyama Prefecture. A pre-festival (宵祭) takes place on May 3, and a post-festival (裏祭) is held on May 5.
== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} consecrated from {{ill|Kamo shrines|en|Kamo shrines|WD=Q1306606|lt=Kamo shrines}}. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurabeuma-shinji|1=Kurabe-uma-e-Jinji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598605}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=賀茂別雷神社}} in Kyoto.
One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019th year -)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/COVID-19 pandemic|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion tier 1]] and 3 {{ill|Shaku|en|Shaku|WD=Q7487087|lt=Shaku}} (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimomura Kamo Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Yabusame|en|Yabusame|WD=Q1886157|lt=Yabusame}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|en|List of festivals in Japan|WD=Q10312376|lt=List of Japanese Festivals}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Festivals of Toyama Prefecture, Toyama Prefecture Festival Research Association, Sakuraōfu-sha, December 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02481-0}} * Traditional Events of Toyama Prefecture, Hidehiro Takahashi and Motomi Urushima, Sakuraōfu-sha, October 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02482-9}} * Festivals of Hokuriku, Hokuriku Electric Power Company Planning and Chūetsu Media Production Division Publishing Department, March 23, 2001, {{ISBN2|4-9980730-2-8}} * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (3): Festivals of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2007 * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (2): Lion Dances of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2006 * Festivals of Imizu, Imizu City Shinminato Museum, October 10, 2008
== External Links == * [https://www.shimomura-kamojinja.com/post_project/0504-2 Kamo Festival (May 4) | Shimomura Kamo Shrine Official Website]

On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019th year -)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/COVID-19 pandemic|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion tier 1]] and 3 {{ill|Shaku|en|Shaku|WD=Q7487087|lt=Shaku|1=Shaku}} (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimomura Kamo Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Yabusame|en|Yabusame|WD=Q1886157|lt=Yabusame|1=Yabusame}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|en|List of festivals in Japan|WD=Q10312376|lt=List of Japanese Festivals|1=List of festivals in Japan}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Festivals of Toyama Prefecture, Toyama Prefecture Festival Research Association, Sakuraōfu-sha, December 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02481-0}} * Traditional Events of Toyama Prefecture, Hidehiro Takahashi and Motomi Urushima, Sakuraōfu-sha, October 25, 1991, {{ISBN2|4-273-02482-9}} * Festivals of Hokuriku, Hokuriku Electric Power Company Planning and Chūetsu Media Production Division Publishing Department, March 23, 2001, {{ISBN2|4-9980730-2-8}} * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (3): Festivals of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2007 * 100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama Series (2): Lion Dances of Toyama, Toyama Prefecture Board of Education, March 2006 * Festivals of Imizu, Imizu City Shinminato Museum, October 10, 2008
== External Links == * [https://www.shimomura-kamojinja.com/post_project/0504-2 Kamo Festival (May 4) | Shimomura Kamo Shrine Official Website]

He rides a saddle-equipped bull, carrying a bow measuring 1 {{ill|Ken|en|Ken|WD=Q226769|lt=Ken|1=Ken}} [[Category:Festivals in Toyama Prefecture]] [[Category:Image Requests for Imizu City]] [[Category:Imizu, Toyama]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

'''Yansanma Festival''' (やんさんま祭り) is a [[yabusame]] event held annually on May 4 at the {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimomura Kamo Shrine|qq=}} in {{ill|Imizu|en|Imizu|WD=Q851411|lt=Imizu City|1=Imizu}}, Toyama Prefecture. A pre-festival (宵祭) takes place on May 3, and a post-festival (裏祭) is held on May 5.
== Overview == The festival is said to have begun when Shimomura Kamo Shrine was established as a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} consecrated from {{ill|Kamo shrines|en|Kamo shrines|WD=Q1306606|lt=Kamo shrines|1=Kamo shrines}}. The name "Yansanma" is derived from a dialectical variation of the word "yabusame." It is believed to have been inspired by the {{ill|Kurabeuma-shinji|ja|競馬会神事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurabeuma-shinji|1=Kurabe-uma-e-Jinji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598605}} held at {{ill|Kamigamo Shrine|ja|上賀茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamigamo Shrine|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=賀茂別雷神社}} in Kyoto.
One of the unique rituals performed at the festival is the "Ushinorishiki" (牛乗式, or "Bull-Riding Ceremony"), a distinctive practice exclusive to this shrine. On March 25, 1967, it was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by Toyama Prefecture.<ref>Visual History of Takaoka, Himi, and Shinminato in 100 Years, published November 27, 1993, Kyodo Shuppan, p. 164.</ref> In 2006, the festival was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Festival Edition."
On April 4, 2020, the festival's main rituals were canceled due to the spread of {{ill|COVID-19 pandemic|ja|新型コロナウイルス感染症の流行 (2019th year -)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/COVID-19 pandemic|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Kitanihon2020.4.5">North Japan Newspaper, "Shimomura Kamo Shrine Yansanma Festival Main Rituals Canceled," April 5, 2020, p. 25.</ref> In 2021, only shrine rituals in the main hall were held, with other main events canceled.<ref name="Kitanihon2021.4.7">North Japan Newspaper, "Yansanma Main Rituals Canceled," April 7, 2021, p. 21.</ref>
== Main Schedule == On May 3, the pre-festival includes simplified versions of the Evening Horse Procession Ritual (宵祭走馬の儀) and Evening Yabusame Ritual (宵祭流鏑馬の儀), which were previously more elaborate.
The main festival on May 4 begins at 8:00 AM with the demon-expelling lion dance, followed by the Horse-Riding Ceremony, Gate-Opening Ceremony, Divine Procession, Sacred Horse Ceremony, Portable Shrine Station Ceremony, Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony), and other rituals. The festival concludes with the Yabusame Ceremony.
The post-festival on May 5 includes the Lion Dance Dedication and the Thanksgiving Ceremony for Festival Completion tier 1]] and 3 {{ill|Shaku|en|Shaku|WD=Q7487087|lt=Shaku|1=Shaku}} (approximately 3.94 meters).
After entering the shrine grounds, he faces the northern direction of the worship hall roof and shoots an arrow while praying for "peace in the realm" and "abundant harvests." The bull, accompanied by many young participants, is then made to circle the portable shrine station three times before being forcibly subdued and made to sit. The ritual symbolizes securing the deity of rice fields and praying for bountiful harvests.
== Yabusame Ceremony == Three mounted participants, adorned with red-and-white ceremonial streamers and wearing traditional attire including woven hats, purple headbands, and velvet sashes, take turns riding along the cedar-lined approach to the shrine. They aim at large targets approximately 5 meters high, shooting three arrows each.
The arrows shot during the Ushinorishiki and Yabusame ceremonies are eagerly collected by spectators, who take them home as talismans for warding off evil and ensuring prosperity.
== Lion Dance == The lion dance features representatives of shrine parishioners, dressed in traditional attire, solemnly carrying small lion heads above their heads as they progress. The dance was selected as part of the "100 Cultural Heritage Selections of Toyama: Lion Dance Edition" in 2005.
* Form: Box Lion (箱獅子) * Type: Processional Lion (行道獅子)
== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimomura Kamo Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Yabusame|en|Yabusame|WD=Q1886157|lt=Yabusame|1=Yabusame}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|en|List of festivals in Japan|WD=Q10312376|lt=List of Japanese Festivals|1=List of festivals in Japan}}

== Shimomura Equestrian Park == Adjacent to Shimomura Kamo Shrine, the park was established in 1986 as a facility for housing and training horses used in the festival's yabusame events. A pasture is located approximately 200 meters from the park.<ref>North Japan Newspaper, "Toyama Parks: Shimomura Equestrian Park," April 16, 2018, p. 7.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Kamo Shrine (Imizu)|lt=Shimomura Kamo Shrine|ja|加茂神社 (射水市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimomura Kamo Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Yabusame|en|Yabusame|WD=Q1886157|lt=Yabusame|1=Yabusame}} * {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|en|List of festivals in Japan|WD=Q10312376|lt=List of Japanese Festivals|1=List of festivals in Japan}}

== Ushinorishiki (Bull-Riding Ceremony) == In this ritual, a young man dressed as "Ōhana" (田の神, deity of rice fields) wears red-faced and large-nosed masks and full armor, including a helmet adorned with a paper peony flower symbolizing the deity.
